title: What is a budget (and why you need one)
description: Give every dollar a job before the month starts.
summaryPoints: A budget gives every dollar a job: needs, wants, or savings
Cover needs first, then savings, then fun — in that order
Fixed expenses stay the same each month; variable ones change
Small leaks sink budgets: $5 a school day is about $100 a month
Leftover money isn't bonus money — it's your emergency fund and goals

content.title: Your money bar
content.body:
Think of a video game where your character has a money bar instead of a health bar. Every choice — new sneakers, saving for a trip, a snack run — moves that bar.

Personal finance is just learning to play that bar well: earning, spending, saving, and eventually investing. Nobody is born good at it. It's a skill, and it's learnable.

content.title: The five jobs of money
content.body:
Almost everything you'll ever do with money lands in one of five buckets:
- **Budgeting** — planning where it goes before it goes
- **Saving** — parking some now for later
- **Investing** — putting money to work so it grows
- **Managing debt** — borrowing on purpose, paying back on time
- **Insurance** — paying a little so a disaster can't wipe you out
content.imageAlt: Income splitting into needs, wants, and savings jars

content.prompt: Match each money job to what it actually means.
Having a budget means cutting all fun spending.
content.explanation: A budget isn't punishment — it plans fun ON PURPOSE so you can enjoy it without guilt.

content.title: Why bother?
content.body:
Managed badly, money runs out before the month does: a broken laptop before exams becomes a crisis, and late fees stack up.

Managed well, the same income pays bills on time, absorbs surprises, and quietly builds toward bigger things — a car, a trip, a degree.

The tool that flips you from the first group to the second is a budget: a plan for your money, made before the month starts.

content.prompt: What's the budget move that makes that survivable?
content.context: Your laptop dies a week before final exams. The repair is $90.
options: Having an emergency cushion saved up
Ignoring it and hoping it fixes itself
Buying a new laptop on a credit card
content.explanation: A budget that saves a little every month turns emergencies into inconveniences instead of debt.

content.title: What's inside a budget
content.body:
Every budget has the same skeleton:
- **Income** — everything coming in each month
- **Fixed expenses** — same every month (rent, internet, subscriptions)
- **Variable expenses** — change month to month (groceries, gas, fun)
- **Savings** — paid like a bill, not an afterthought

Example month: income $3,000. Fixed costs $1,740, variable $300. That's $2,040 out — leaving $960 to put to work.

content.prompt: Income $3,000. Total expenses $2,040. The leftover is $___.
content.explanation: $3,000 − $2,040 = $960. That leftover isn't 'free money' — it's what funds your goals.

content.title: The order of operations
content.body:
When money lands, it gets spent in this order:
- **Needs first** — rent, food, transport, school stuff
- **Savings second** — even 10% counts; small is fine, skipping isn't
- **Wants last** — fun money you can spend guilt-free

And track what you spend for at least one week — including the small stuff. Tiny purchases are where budgets quietly die.

content.instructions: One word ruins this sentence. Tap it.
content.sentence: In a good budget, every dollar you earn gets a vacation.
content.explanation: Money doesn't just disappear — it goes where you tell it. Give every dollar a job.

content.prompt: A $5 snack every school day (about 20 days). Slide to what that costs per month.
content.explanation: $5 × 20 school days = $100 a month. In a year that's $1,200 — a laptop, gone in crumbs.

content.context: Surprise! Your bike needs a $50 repair. You have $60 of flexible money left this month, and your $50 concert savings is already set aside.
options: Pay the $50 repair from the $60 — fun money takes the hit
Put the repair on a credit card and pay it 'later'
Skip the repair and walk for a month
content.explanation: Surprises come out of flexible spending first, so your goals stay untouched. That's the budget absorbing the hit for you.

content.prompt: Your first budget. You earn $500 this month. Cover every need exactly, put at least $50 toward the concert fund, then treat yourself with what's left.
content.explanation: Needs ($340) first, savings goal ($50) second, and the last $110 is genuinely guilt-free fun.

content.context: You want to save $50 for a concert, but after bills you only have $30 left this month.
options: Save the $30 now, save the last $20 next month
Borrow $20 from a friend to hit $50 today
content.explanation: Partial progress beats no progress, and borrowing for a want builds exactly the wrong habit. Consistency is the whole game.
